---
title: "Understand "
output: html_document
date: "2026-02-08"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, fig.width = 10, fig.height = 8)
```

```{r fig.width=10, fig.height=6}

# --- Geometric Visualization: Dual Ellipses + Gradient Field ---

# 1. Setup Matrix A = QDQ'
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
theta <- 30 * pi / 180  # Rotate by 30 degrees
R <- matrix(c(cos(theta), sin(theta), -sin(theta), cos(theta)), 
            nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)

# Eigenvalues (D): lambda1=4 (Stiff/Short), lambda2=1 (Flat/Long)
lambda <- c(4, 1)
D <- diag(lambda)
Q <- t(R) 
A <- Q %*% D %*% t(Q)

# 2. Generate Points
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
theta_seq <- seq(0, 2*pi, length.out = 100)
unit_circle <- rbind(cos(theta_seq), sin(theta_seq))

# A) Contour Ellipse Points: x'Ax = 1
sqrt_inv_D <- diag(1 / sqrt(lambda))
A_inv_sqrt <- Q %*% sqrt_inv_D %*% t(Q)
points_contour <- A_inv_sqrt %*% unit_circle

# B) Gradient Ellipse Points: g = Ax
points_gradient <- A %*% points_contour

# 3. Plotting
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Determine limits to fit both ellipses
max_range <- max(abs(points_gradient)) * 1.2

plot(NA, xlim=c(-max_range, max_range), ylim=c(-max_range, max_range), 
     asp=1, xlab="x1", ylab="x2", 
     main="Dual Geometry: Contour, Gradient Locus, and Normal Field")
grid()
abline(h=0, v=0, col="gray85", lty=2)

# Draw the Shapes (Loci)
lines(points_contour[1,], points_contour[2,], col="gray50", lwd=2, lty=1)
text(points_contour[1,5], points_contour[2,5], "Contour (x)", 
     col="gray50", pos=2, cex=0.8, font=3)

lines(points_gradient[1,], points_gradient[2,], col="gray50", lwd=2, lty=2)
text(points_gradient[1,25], points_gradient[2,25], "Gradient Locus (Ax)", 
     col="gray50", pos=4, cex=0.8, font=3)

# 4. Visualizing Vectors and Field
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
sample_indices <- seq(1, 100, by=8) 
n_samples <- length(sample_indices)
vector_colors <- rainbow(n_samples, end=0.85) 

for(k in 1:n_samples) {
  i <- sample_indices[k]
  col_vec <- vector_colors[k]
  
  x_pt <- points_contour[,i]  # Point on ellipse
  g_pt <- points_gradient[,i] # Gradient vector
  
  # A) Position Vector x (Dashed, from Origin)
  arrows(0, 0, x_pt[1], x_pt[2], col=col_vec, 
         length=0, lty=2, lwd=1)
  
  # B) Gradient Vector Ax (Solid, from Origin - showing the Dual Shape)
  arrows(0, 0, g_pt[1], g_pt[2], col=col_vec, 
         length=0.1, lwd=2)
  
  # C) Shrunken Gradient Field (Starting at x - showing Orthogonality)
  # Scale factor for the field arrows so they don't look huge
  field_scale <- 0.25 
  arrows(x_pt[1], x_pt[2], 
         x_pt[1] + g_pt[1]*field_scale, x_pt[2] + g_pt[2]*field_scale, 
         col=col_vec, length=0.08, lwd=1.5)
}

# Draw Principal Axes
axis_scale <- 2.2
arrows(0, 0, axis_scale*Q[1,1], axis_scale*Q[2,1], col="black", lwd=1)
text(axis_scale*Q[1,1], axis_scale*Q[2,1], "q1 (Stiff)", col="black", pos=4, cex=0.7)
arrows(0, 0, axis_scale*Q[1,2], axis_scale*Q[2,2], col="black", lwd=1)
text(axis_scale*Q[1,2], axis_scale*Q[2,2], "q2 (Flat)", col="black", pos=4, cex=0.7)

legend("topleft", legend=c("Position x", "Gradient Ax (at Origin)", "Gradient Field (at Point)"),
       col=c("black", "black", "black"), lty=c(2, 1, 1), pch=c(NA, NA, NA), 
       lwd=c(1, 2, 1.5), bg="white", cex=0.8)
```

```{r}
# --- Optimization: Gradient Descent vs. Newton's Method ---

# 1. Setup Matrix A (Stiff Valley)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
theta <- 30 * pi / 180 
R <- matrix(c(cos(theta), sin(theta), -sin(theta), cos(theta)), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
lambda <- c(10, 1) # Higher stiffness ratio (10:1) to exaggerate the zig-zag
D <- diag(lambda)
A <- Q <- t(R)
A <- Q %*% D %*% t(Q)

# Function to minimize: f(x) = 0.5 * x'Ax
f <- function(x) as.numeric(0.5 * t(x) %*% A %*% x)
grad_f <- function(x) A %*% x  # Gradient = Ax

# 2. Run Optimization Algorithms
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
start_point <- c(3, 1.5) # Start somewhere on the "flat" side

# A) Gradient Descent (Zig-Zag)
path_gd <- matrix(start_point, ncol=2)
x_curr <- start_point
learning_rate <- 0.15 # Small step size needed for stability

for(i in 1:15) {
  g <- grad_f(x_curr)
  x_curr <- x_curr - learning_rate * g
  path_gd <- rbind(path_gd, as.vector(x_curr))
}

# B) Newton's Method (Straight Shot)
# Step = - Hessian^-1 * Gradient = - A^-1 * (Ax) = -x
path_newton <- matrix(start_point, ncol=2)
# One step of Newton leads exactly to origin (0,0) for quadratic
path_newton <- rbind(path_newton, c(0,0)) 

# 3. Plotting
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Setup contour plot
x_seq <- seq(-4, 4, length.out=100)
y_seq <- seq(-4, 4, length.out=100)
z <- outer(x_seq, y_seq, Vectorize(function(a,b) f(c(a,b))))

plot(NA, xlim=c(-1, 3.5), ylim=c(-1, 3), asp=1, 
     xlab="x1", ylab="x2", main="Why Gradient != Direction to Center")
contour(x_seq, y_seq, z, nlevels=15, add=TRUE, col="gray80")
grid()

# Draw Paths
lines(path_gd[,1], path_gd[,2], col="red", lwd=2, type="b", pch=19, cex=0.8)
text(path_gd[2,1], path_gd[2,2], "Gradient Descent\n(Follows Ax)", 
     col="red", pos=4, cex=0.8)

lines(path_newton[,1], path_newton[,2], col="blue", lwd=2, type="b", pch=19, cex=0.8)
text(1.5, 0.5, "Newton's Method\n(Follows A^-1 Ax)", 
     col="blue", pos=1, cex=0.8)

legend("topright", legend=c("Gradient Path (Zig-Zag)", "Newton Path (Direct)"),
       col=c("red", "blue"), lwd=2, bg="white")
```


```{r}
# --- The Orthogonality Trap: Steepest Descent with Exact Line Search ---

# 1. Setup Matrix A (Stiff Valley)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
theta <- 30 * pi / 180 
R <- matrix(c(cos(theta), sin(theta), -sin(theta), cos(theta)), 
            nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)

# Condition Number = 10 (Stiff, ill-conditioned)
# This creates a long, narrow valley
lambda <- c(10, 1) 
A <- R %*% diag(lambda) %*% t(R)

# 2. Define Helper Functions
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# The Objective Function: f(x) = 0.5 * x'Ax
f <- function(x) {
  as.numeric(0.5 * t(x) %*% A %*% x)
}

# The Gradient: grad(f) = Ax
grad_f <- function(x) {
  as.vector(A %*% x)
}

# Exact Line Search Formula for Quadratics
# We want to minimize f(x - alpha*g) with respect to alpha.
# The analytic solution is: alpha = (g'g) / (g'Ag)
get_exact_step_size <- function(x, g) {
  numerator <- sum(g * g)          # g transpose g
  denominator <- as.numeric(t(g) %*% A %*% g) # g transpose A g
  return(numerator / denominator)
}

# 3. Run Optimization
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Start point: deliberately off the "good" axis to trigger zig-zag
x_curr <- c(2.0, 1.5) 
path <- matrix(x_curr, ncol=2)

# Run 10 iterations
for(i in 1:10) {
  g <- grad_f(x_curr)
  
  # Check for convergence (gradient close to 0)
  if(sqrt(sum(g^2)) < 1e-6) break
  
  # Calculate EXACT step size
  alpha <- get_exact_step_size(x_curr, g)
  
  # Update Position
  # Note: Direction is -g (Steepest Descent)
  x_next <- x_curr - alpha * g
  
  # Store and update
  path <- rbind(path, x_next)
  x_curr <- x_next
}

# 4. Plotting
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Setup contour plot grid
x_seq <- seq(-1, 2.5, length.out=100)
y_seq <- seq(-1, 2.5, length.out=100)
z <- outer(x_seq, y_seq, Vectorize(function(a,b) f(c(a,b))))

plot(NA, xlim=c(-0.5, 2.5), ylim=c(-0.5, 2.5), asp=1, 
     xlab="x1", ylab="x2", 
     main="The Orthogonality Trap: Exact Line Search")

# Draw Contours
contour(x_seq, y_seq, z, nlevels=20, add=TRUE, col="gray70")
points(0, 0, pch=3, lwd=2, col="black") # Minimum

# Draw the Zig-Zag Path
lines(path[,1], path[,2], col="blue", type="b", pch=19, lwd=1.5, cex=0.8)

# 5. Visualize the Orthogonality (Right Angles)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Let's draw a little red square at the 2nd turn to prove it's 90 degrees
p1 <- path[2,] # The corner point
p0 <- path[1,] # Previous point
p2 <- path[3,] # Next point

# Vectors entering and leaving the corner
v_in <- p1 - p0
v_out <- p2 - p1

# Normalize for drawing the marker
len <- 0.15
u_in <- v_in / sqrt(sum(v_in^2)) * len
u_out <- v_out / sqrt(sum(v_out^2)) * len

# Draw the right-angle marker
segments(p1[1]-u_in[1], p1[2]-u_in[2], 
         p1[1]-u_in[1]+u_out[1], p1[2]-u_in[2]+u_out[2], col="red", lwd=2)
segments(p1[1]+u_out[1], p1[2]+u_out[2], 
         p1[1]-u_in[1]+u_out[1], p1[2]-u_in[2]+u_out[2], col="red", lwd=2)

text(1.5, 2.0, "Every turn is exactly 90°\n(Orthogonal to previous gradient)", 
     col="blue", pos=1, cex=0.8)

legend("topright", legend=c("Steepest Descent Path", "90° Turn Marker"),
       col=c("blue", "red"), lty=1, lwd=2, bg="white")
```


```{r}
# --- BFGS Visualization: Learning the Geometry ---

# 1. Setup Matrix A (The "Truth")
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
theta <- 30 * pi / 180 
R <- matrix(c(cos(theta), sin(theta), -sin(theta), cos(theta)), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
# Stiffness = 15 (Significant ill-conditioning)
lambda <- c(15, 1) 
A <- R %*% diag(lambda) %*% t(R)

# Helper Functions
f <- function(x) as.numeric(0.5 * t(x) %*% A %*% x)
grad_f <- function(x) as.vector(A %*% x)

# Exact Line Search for Quadratic Problems
# Returns optimal step size alpha for direction p
get_step_size <- function(x, p) {
  g <- grad_f(x)
  numerator <- -sum(g * p)        # -g'p
  denominator <- as.numeric(t(p) %*% A %*% p) # p'Ap
  if(denominator < 1e-8) return(0)
  return(numerator / denominator)
}

# 2. Run Gradient Descent (The "Dumb" Baseline)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
x0 <- c(2.0, 1.5) # Start point
path_gd <- matrix(x0, ncol=2)
x_curr <- x0

for(i in 1:10) {
  g <- grad_f(x_curr)
  if(sqrt(sum(g^2)) < 1e-4) break
  
  d <- -g # Direction = Negative Gradient
  alpha <- get_step_size(x_curr, d)
  x_curr <- x_curr + alpha * d
  path_gd <- rbind(path_gd, x_curr)
}

# 3. Run BFGS (The "Smart" Learner)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
path_bfgs <- matrix(x0, ncol=2)
hessian_shapes <- list() # To store the H matrices for plotting
x_curr <- x0
n <- 2
H <- diag(n) # Initial Inverse Hessian Estimate = Identity (Circle)
I_mat <- diag(n)

# Store initial shape
hessian_shapes[[1]] <- list(center=x0, H=H)

for(i in 1:6) {
  g <- grad_f(x_curr)
  if(sqrt(sum(g^2)) < 1e-6) break
  
  # 1. Determine Direction: p = -H * g
  p <- as.vector(-H %*% g)
  
  # 2. Line Search
  alpha <- get_step_size(x_curr, p)
  
  # 3. Update Position
  s <- alpha * p          # The step vector (dx)
  x_next <- x_curr + s
  
  # 4. BFGS Update of Hessian approximation
  y <- grad_f(x_next) - g # The gradient change vector (dg)
  
  # Curvature condition check
  ys <- sum(y * s)
  if(ys > 1e-8) {
    rho <- 1 / ys
    term1 <- I_mat - rho * (s %*% t(y))
    term2 <- I_mat - rho * (y %*% t(s))
    # The BFGS Formula
    H <- term1 %*% H %*% term2 + (rho * (s %*% t(s)))
  }
  
  # Store path and shape
  path_bfgs <- rbind(path_bfgs, x_next)
  hessian_shapes[[i+1]] <- list(center=x_next, H=H)
  x_curr <- x_next
}

# 4. Plotting
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Setup grid for contours
limit <- 2.5
x_seq <- seq(-1, limit, length.out=100)
y_seq <- seq(-1, limit, length.out=100)
z <- outer(x_seq, y_seq, Vectorize(function(a,b) f(c(a,b))))

plot(NA, xlim=c(-0.5, limit), ylim=c(-0.5, limit), asp=1, 
     xlab="x1", ylab="x2", 
     main="BFGS in Action: Learning the Inverse Hessian")

# Draw True Contours (Gray)
contour(x_seq, y_seq, z, nlevels=15, add=TRUE, col="gray80", lwd=1)
points(0, 0, pch=3, lwd=2) # Minimum

# Draw Gradient Descent Path (Red Zig-Zag)
lines(path_gd[,1], path_gd[,2], col="red", type="b", pch=19, cex=0.7, lwd=1.5)
text(path_gd[2,1], path_gd[2,2], "GD (Zig-Zag)", col="red", pos=4, cex=0.8)

# Draw BFGS Path (Blue Curve)
lines(path_bfgs[,1], path_bfgs[,2], col="blue", type="b", pch=19, cex=0.7, lwd=2)
text(path_bfgs[2,1], path_bfgs[2,2], "BFGS (Direct)", col="blue", pos=2, cex=0.8)

# 5. Visualize the "Learned Geometry" (Green Ellipses)
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# For each step, we draw the ellipse corresponding to x' H_inv x = c
# Since H is the inverse Hessian, its eigenvectors define the shape axes
# Axis length is proportional to sqrt(eigenvalues of H)

draw_ellipse_from_H <- function(center, H, scale=0.4, col="darkgreen") {
  # Eigendecomposition of H
  eig <- eigen(H)
  vals <- eig$values
  vecs <- eig$vectors
  
  # Generate unit circle points
  angles <- seq(0, 2*pi, length.out=100)
  circle <- rbind(cos(angles), sin(angles))
  
  # Transform: x = Center + Scale * V * sqrt(D) * circle
  # This visualizes the shape of the uncertainty/metric H
  transform <- vecs %*% diag(sqrt(vals)) %*% t(vecs)
  points <- transform %*% circle * scale
  
  lines(points[1,] + center[1], points[2,] + center[2], col=col, lty=2, lwd=1.5)
}

# Draw the ellipses for the first few steps
for(i in 1:min(4, length(hessian_shapes))) {
  shape <- hessian_shapes[[i]]
  draw_ellipse_from_H(shape$center, shape$H)
  text(shape$center[1], shape$center[2]-0.2, paste0("H", i-1), 
       col="darkgreen", cex=0.7)
}

legend("topright", legend=c("True Contours (Gray)", "GD Path", "BFGS Path", "Learned Metric (H)"),
       col=c("gray80", "red", "blue", "darkgreen"), lty=c(1,1,1,2), lwd=2, bg="white")
```
